title: The Single-Batch Baseline for Text
concept: Like CNNs (callback to Unit 129), check if your Transformer can perfectly memorize 4 text sequences; failure indicates a core backpropagation or loss bug.

exercises.prompt: What kind of dataset do we use for a single-batch baseline test?

options.text: A tiny dataset of about 4 sentences
A massive dataset with billions of words

exercises.explain: A single-batch baseline uses a tiny, simplified dataset (usually just a few sentences) to quickly test if the model's training loop and code are free of major bugs.

cards.title: Is Your Code Actually Working?
cards.body: You've just built a massive Transformer and you're ready to train it on gigabytes of text. But wait! How do you know your code actually works, and that you won't waste days training a broken model? You start with a tiny test.

cards.title: What is the Single-Batch Baseline?
cards.body: The Single-Batch Baseline is a debugging technique where you feed your Transformer a tiny dataset (like just 4 sentences) and train it for many steps. Because the dataset is so small, the model should easily achieve 100% accuracy and near-zero loss. If it cannot, you have a bug in your code.

cards.title: The Tiny Parrot Test
cards.body: Imagine you're trying to teach a parrot to repeat just one specific, short sentence. If the parrot cannot learn that single sentence after hours of practicing, there is no way it will ever hold a conversation. Memorizing one sentence is the bare minimum test to see if the parrot's ears and vocal cords are working at all!

cards.title: Testing with 4 Sentences
cards.body:
Before training a GPT model on the entire internet, you feed it only these 4 lines:
1. 'The cat sat.'
2. 'Dogs love bones.'
3. 'Birds can fly.'
4. 'Fish live deep.'

If your code is correct, the model will quickly learn to predict these exact words perfectly. If the loss stays high, you know there is a bug to fix before spending money on big GPU runs!

cards.title: Myth: More Data Fixes Everything
cards.body: Many beginners think that if their model fails to learn a tiny batch, they just need to add more data. In reality, if a model cannot memorize 4 simple sentences, giving it 4 million sentences will only make it fail faster. Fix the code bugs first!

cards.title: Debugging Smart vs. Wild Guessing
cards.body: Before running expensive training on giant datasets, verify your setup with a tiny test.

left.text: Training on a giant dataset immediately. If there is a bug, you waste time and money without knowing why it's failing.

right.text: Training on 4 sentences first. If loss goes to zero, your code works and you're ready for the big dataset.

exercises.prompt: When you run the single-batch baseline on a healthy Transformer, what should happen to the loss?

options.text: It should quickly reach near-zero loss and memorize the sentences.
It should generalize and start writing new stories immediately.
It should take several days of training to see any progress.
It should automatically shut down to save electricity.

exercises.explain: A healthy neural network has enough capacity to easily overfit and memorize a tiny dataset of 4 sentences. If it fails, something is broken in the code.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If your model fails to memorize a single batch of 4 sentences, there is likely a bug in your code.
exercises.explain: If a model cannot memorize a tiny dataset, it usually means there is a bug in how gradients flow or how the loss is calculated.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of the single-batch baseline test.
exercises.sentence: We use a single tiny batch to make sure our Transformer can perfectly [blank] a few sentences.
exercises.explain: The single-batch test checks if the model can easily memorize a tiny amount of data, proving the math and code are working.

exercises.prompt: Which AI assistant correctly describes the purpose and outcome of the single-batch baseline?

options.text: AI: "I run a single-batch test to verify my code. If the model memorizes the 4 sentences, my loss and gradients are likely working correctly." Byzantium: "Correct!"
AI: "If my Transformer cannot learn 4 sentences, it means the dataset is too small, so I should immediately add 10,000 more sentences." Byzantium: "Correct!"
AI: "The single-batch test is the final step of training where we teach the model how to chat with real humans." Byzantium: "Correct!"

exercises.explain: The second option is incorrect because a failing single-batch test means the code has a bug, not that the model needs more data. The third option is incorrect because the single-batch test is a debugging step, not the final training phase.

exercises.prompt: You are training a new Transformer on a tiny batch of 4 sentences, but the loss refuses to go down to zero even after 500 epochs. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The dataset is too small and needs millions of more sentences to learn.
The learning rate is slightly too high for the final epoch.
There is a bug in the model code, such as an incorrect loss calculation or a broken gradient path.
The Transformer is running out of memory because the dataset is too big.

exercises.explain: If a Transformer cannot memorize a tiny single batch of text, there is a fundamental bug in the model's architecture, loss computation, or optimization setup. Training on the full dataset will fail because the model's learning pipeline is broken.
